description: 70F near-syncope with one CHESS criterion (mildly abnormal EKG) — borderline SF Syncope
transcript:
Dr. Hayes: What brought you in?
Patient: I almost passed out this morning. I was standing up from the couch and I got really lightheaded, the room started to gray out. I sat back down and it passed in maybe thirty seconds.
Dr. Hayes: Did you actually lose consciousness?
Patient: No, I sat down before that happened. But I was close.
Dr. Hayes: Has this happened before?
Patient: Not like this. I've gotten a little dizzy before standing, but never this bad.
Dr. Hayes: Any chest pain, palpitations, shortness of breath?
Patient: No chest pain. Maybe a little short of breath when I walk to the mailbox lately, but I figured I was just out of shape.
Dr. Hayes: Any history of heart failure?
Patient: No.
Dr. Hayes: Past medical?
Patient: High blood pressure, on amlodipine. Cholesterol, on a statin.
Dr. Hayes: Family history of sudden cardiac death?
Patient: No.
Nurse: HR 64, BP 128/74, sat 97, temp normal. Hematocrit pending.
Dr. Hayes: Let me look at the EKG. Sinus, rate 64, but I'm seeing some new T-wave inversions in the lateral leads compared to her old EKG from last year. That's new.
Dr. Hayes: Lungs clear. Heart regular. No leg edema. Neuro intact.
Dr. Hayes: This is technically near-syncope, but with new EKG changes I'm not comfortable calling this benign. Let me think through the risk-stratification.

groundTruth.notes: Edge case. Some interpretations of SF Syncope require true LOC; many EM docs apply the framework to near-syncope with similar features. Here there is a positive CHESS criterion (new EKG changes) and the doctor is explicitly entering risk-stratification mode. Surfacing the rule with the abnormal_ekg pre-filled is decision-relevant. The remaining CHESS criteria should be marked as missing — especially the SOB question, which the doctor only got a soft answer to.
